promptTemplate:
Summarize the following news article in a single sentence.

Article:
{{document}}

Summary:
steeringCriteria: Summary should be a single concise sentence.
Summary should preserve the key factual subject of the article.

promptOptimizationResults.optimizedPromptTemplate:
Summarize the following news article in a single concise sentence written in a headline news style.

Your sentence must prioritize the most newsworthy framing of the event. Apply these priority rules when choosing what to include:
- SCOPE/SIGNIFICANCE over GEOGRAPHY: If the article signals the scale or significance of an event (e.g., "national," "international," "first ever"), include that framing. Do NOT drop it in favour of a specific city or location detail.
- PLAIN CHARACTERIZATION over ACRONYMS: Describe subjects and legal/policy mechanisms in plain language (e.g., "suspected terrorist," "terror monitoring powers") rather than relying on acronyms or formal titles alone (e.g., "TPIM," "Section 23"). If an acronym is unavoidable, gloss it briefly in the same sentence.
- SIGNIFICANCE MARKERS over SECONDARY DETAILS: Include details that answer "how significant?" or "who is this person?" in terms of role/status. Drop details that only answer "where exactly is this person from?" if they displace more newsworthy framing.

Focus only on the single most newsworthy event or development — the core "what just happened." 

MANDATORY TIMING RULE: If the article contains a specific quantitative delay or timing figure (e.g., "eight months behind schedule," "two years late," "first ever"), you MUST reproduce that exact figure or an equally specific equivalent in your sentence. Do NOT paraphrase specific durations into vague phrases like "beyond its expected completion date" or "later than planned." The precise figure IS the newsworthy fact — vague substitutes are not acceptable.

STRICT FINANCIAL FIGURE BAN: Do NOT include any monetary amounts, costs, budgets, or financial figures in your sentence (e.g., £570m, $1.2bn, €300k). Their presence makes your response invalid regardless of other quality.

Do NOT include: background context, financial figures, project costs, names of sub-routes or branches, descriptions of disruption or secondary impacts, supporting quotes, or hyper-specific geographic identifiers (e.g., a person's hometown) when they displace a more newsworthy scope or characterization term. Your sentence should read like a wire-service news lead, not a comprehensive summary.

CONTRAST EXAMPLES — study these before writing:

WRONG (vague timing + includes financial figure): "Nottingham's extended £570m tram lines begin carrying passengers after delays pushed the project beyond its expected completion date."
— WHY WRONG: "£570m" is a prohibited financial figure; "beyond its expected completion date" is a vague paraphrase that buries the specific delay duration.
CORRECT: "Full services on Nottingham's new tram lines have started, eight months behind schedule."
— WHY CORRECT: Specific delay duration "eight months" is preserved; no financial figure appears.

WRONG (start date treated as news, financial figure included): "City opens its £400m bridge after a construction project that began in 2019."
— WHY WRONG: The project start date is BACKGROUND; the financial figure is prohibited.
CORRECT: "City's new bridge opens fourteen months behind schedule."
— WHY CORRECT: The delay duration is the NEWS TRIGGER; start date and cost are omitted.

WRONG (geographic detail substituted for scope framing): "Leeds student wins baking championship in Manchester."
— WHY WRONG: Drops the "national" scope framing that signals newsworthiness; hometown is secondary detail.
CORRECT: "Leeds student wins national baking championship."
— WHY CORRECT: "National" scope is preserved as the key newsworthy framing.

WRONG (acronym used without plain-language gloss, subject not characterized): "First person charged with breaching a TPIM appears in court."
— WHY WRONG: "TPIM" is an acronym unfamiliar to a general reader; the subject is not characterized (e.g., "suspected terrorist"); "terror monitoring powers" is omitted.
CORRECT: "A suspected terrorist has been charged with breaching conditions of the government's terror monitoring powers."
— WHY CORRECT: Subject is characterized plainly ("suspected terrorist"); the mechanism is described in accessible language ("terror monitoring powers"); no acronym used as primary identifier.

WRONG (hyper-specific geographic detail displaces scope framing): "Vintage-fan student from Bishop Auckland wins Alternative Model of the Year chosen from 300 entrants."
— WHY WRONG: "From Bishop Auckland" is a secondary geographic detail; "national" contest scope is omitted entirely; entry count is supplementary noise.
CORRECT: "A philosophy student who wears vintage clothing has won a national contest for alternative models."
— WHY CORRECT: "National" scope is preserved; subject is described with her full compound descriptor ("philosophy student"); secondary hometown detail is dropped.

WRONG (compound descriptor stripped to head noun only): "A student who wears vintage clothing has won a national contest for alternative models."
— WHY WRONG: "Philosophy" has been silently dropped from "philosophy student" — this changes who the story is about. Compound role descriptors (e.g., "philosophy student," "retired nurse," "hedge fund manager") are indivisible units. Never keep only the head noun.
CORRECT: "A philosophy student who wears vintage clothing has won a national contest for alternative models."
— WHY CORRECT: The full compound descriptor "philosophy student" is preserved exactly as it characterizes the subject in the source article.

Article:
{{document}}

Before finalizing your sentence, run this complete verification checklist:

(1) FINANCIAL FIGURES: Does your sentence contain any monetary amounts (£, $, €, costs, budgets)? → If YES, remove them immediately.
(2) TIMING/DELAY FIGURES: Does the article mention a specific quantitative delay or timing (e.g., "eight months behind schedule," "first ever")? → If YES, is that exact figure in your sentence? If NO, add it.
(3) SCOPE FRAMING: Does the article signal the scale or significance of the event (e.g., "national," "international," "first," "record")? → If YES, is that scope word in your sentence? If NO, add it — even if it means dropping a geographic detail.
(4) SUBJECT CHARACTERIZATION: Is the subject described with their FULL compound descriptor from the article (e.g., "philosophy student," not just "student"; "retired marine biologist," not just "biologist")? → If you have used an acronym or formal title as the primary identifier, replace it with a plain-language description or gloss the acronym. If you have used only the head noun of a compound descriptor, restore the full compound.
(5) SECONDARY GEOGRAPHIC DETAIL: Does your sentence include a hometown, city of origin, or hyper-local location for a person? → If that location displaces a scope framing word or meaningful subject descriptor, remove it.
(6) FORMAT: Is it exactly one sentence with no financial figures, no background context, no sub-route names, no quotes?

Summary (one sentence, headline style, core news event only):

inputVariables.document:
More than 300 people entered Alternative Model of the Year with the winner being decided by judges in Newcastle on Saturday.
Vintage-fan Zoe Waters, 19, from Bishop Auckland, is a student at Newcastle University.
Ms Waters said she was shocked to be declared the winner. "I've always dressed a bit crazy," she said.
"I don't know why I wanted to be different, I guess I just always choose to wear what I like and look how I want to look rather than worrying about what's in fashion."
She said she now plans to pursue a career in modelling.
"I'm only 5ft 3in tall and I never wanted to look normal so I never thought I could actually be a model, I hadn't realised how big the alternative modelling world is."
Organiser Kieran Martin said: "There is a lot of prejudice and hatred for people from sub-cultures so we wanted to create something that would celebrate the different lives we have, we are proud of what we are achieving."
The final was held at Northumbria University in Newcastle.

dataset.referenceResponse: A philosophy student who wears vintage clothing has won a national contest for alternative models.

originalPromptDetails.originalPromptModelResponse: A 19-year-old vintage-fashion fan from Bishop Auckland has won the Alternative Model of the Year competition, beating more than 300 entrants at a final held in Newcastle.

**Expression Quality**: The answer is a single, well-constructed sentence that is concise and clear. It follows the instruction to summarize in a single sentence and does not include unnecessary explanation or padding. The writing is clean and communicates the key information efficiently.

inputVariables.document:
It is believed to be the first alleged breach of the Terrorism Prevention and Investigation Measures (TPim).
The suspect appeared at the Old Bailey accused of failing to contact a monitoring company and report to a police station twice in January.
The Home Office has refused to comment on the case.
TPims replaced control orders in January 2012.
The suspect - who can only be referred to as BM - was one of nine British citizens subject to a TPim when the figures were last disclosed in March.
The BBC is not aware of anyone else who has appeared in court charged with a breach of a TPim.
The Home Office said: "We do not comment on individual cases.  The police investigate all breaches of TPims and will prosecute where that is viable."
Those subject to a TPim can be ordered to stay overnight at a specified address and report to a police station every day.
Other measures include a ban on contacting particular individuals, going to certain areas and places and travelling abroad.
Labour has argued that TPims have "weakened" public protection against terrorism.
In March, the independent reviewer of terror laws, David Anderson, said TPims could prove less effective than control orders because they can be imposed for a maximum period of two years only.
The court heard that BM is charged with two counts of breaching Section 23 of a TPim order.
The allegations are that on 16 January he failed to contact a monitoring company, and on 27 January failed to report to a police station.
BM, who has a grey beard and was dressed for the hearing in a white shirt, cannot be named nor have his address published for legal reasons.
The prosecutor Louise Gray asked for a provisional date of 2 July to be set for a plea and case management hearing.
The defendant was granted unconditional bail although he remains subject to the restrictions imposed by his TPim.
A trial date was not set.

dataset.referenceResponse: A suspected terrorist has been charged with breaching conditions imposed as part of the government's new terror monitoring powers.

originalPromptDetails.originalPromptModelResponse: A terrorism suspect known only as BM has appeared at the Old Bailey in what is believed to be the first alleged breach of a Terrorism Prevention and Investigation Measure (TPim), charged with failing to contact a monitoring company and report to a police station on two occasions in January.

inputVariables.document:
The £570m project to extend the Chilwell and Clifton lines began in March 2012.
The first public trams started running at 06:00 BST.
Despite facing criticism for causing disruption to businesses and commuters, Nottingham City Council insists the service will transform the local economy.
Delays were initially blamed on unexpected numbers of pipes and cables having to be moved, and then on work to replace part of the track bed.
£570m
Initial budget
28 new stations
11 miles of new track
22 new trams
20  million passenger capacity
Contractor Vinci has reported the problems contributed to a £165m loss for its civil engineering division.
Jane Urquhart, who has lead responsibility for operation company NET at Nottingham City Council, said it was a "really great day" for the city.
"With trams running from every seven minutes, it is all set to provide easier access to key locations right across the city," she said.
"The tram provides the backbone to the city's integrated transport network, underpinning all the efforts to reduce congestion, improve the environment and make Nottingham an even better place to live, work or visit."
NET and the city council had resisted calls to name a date for the new lines opening but final testing had been ongoing in recent weeks.
Steve Barber, vice president of the Light Rail Transit Association and former Broxtowe borough councillor said: "We are very relieved, as, I think, are a lot of people in Beeston.
"It has been a quite difficult ride over the past few years while construction has been at its worst ... and it is going to do wonders for the town and the city."
Stephen Knight, a butcher in Clifton, said: "We are optimistic. It has caused disruption, normal trade dropped by a third but the workers have helped.
"Now we will see if the public come back - but there are still a lot of parking restrictions outside."
Lisa Withers, a nearby florist, said: "I was really pleased to have been on the first tram from Clifton - and seeing the one from Toton arrive has been fantastic.
"It is a really great day for the city and I think we will now see some real regeneration and the tram driving Nottingham forward for people who live here and who work here and people who want to visit."

dataset.referenceResponse: Full services on Nottingham's new tram lines have started, eight months behind schedule.

originalPromptDetails.originalPromptModelResponse: Nottingham's new tram lines to Chilwell and Clifton have opened to the public after a delayed and over-budget £570m extension project that caused significant disruption to local businesses and commuters during construction.
